## [00:00 - 03:01]

conversion paths. Now this is one of the most underrated parts of Google Analytics. This is something I've been asking Facebook for many many years to install into ads manager. They can, they've got the data but they haven't. Now what top conversion paths shows you is depending on how you view the data, what touch points are the most popular when it comes to user journeys. So if I illustrate this in a simple to view way. So again I'm going to type in source and medium so that I can actually see what's happening. So let's, okay we've got source medium path here. So I'm gonna, so here for example I'm looking at, in fact I'm going to deselect the conversion segment. So by the way just that's a good tip just to make sure that you are looking at the data you expect. I had a conversion segment running before I started this video. I've taken it out and you can see where to take that out. So I'm looking at all of the data here now. So previously I just showed Facebook. I'm now showing all the data. So I can see the most popular paths over the last 30 days have been to Google paid clicks, a Google paid click and then a text ad, then Google ad and then direct etc etc. And then if I want to I can go down and look at Facebook and CPC. So there's two ways of doing that. I can add a conversion segment. So this has been set up already to show only source medium contains Facebook and CPC which is how we've set up our UTMs. You can also do it in the view below as well by typing the same thing in to your advanced segments. So what I can do is if I'm looking at realistically I just want to have a top level view of kind of what's happening. I can see this or I can go to advanced and I could say so medium let's go into source medium path and go to advance, contain in Facebook CPC and that should give us just our Facebook ads data. So it's two ways to cut the data in the same way. So now what I'm looking at is when I'm looking at my conversion paths I'm looking at just Facebook data here and what's cool is if I type in campaign path I can see which specific Facebook campaigns are contributing to conversion. So it's actually taken out my advanced breakdown which is fine. I'll just put the segment back in just to ensure that doesn't happen again but what I can do here is actually break down my campaign data by just what's happening from Facebook. So this just tells Google Analytics only show me the campaign path

## [03:01 - 06:05]

if it came from Facebook. So I can see the majority of my conversions came from someone clicking this specific campaign and then clicking something which wasn't tracked. Now if it's not tracked it means it was outside of Facebook or it was organic or you know there was no UTM parameter tracked but some interesting things are further down. So for example the sixth most popular journey again in this time period is that someone clicked my cold campaign and then they clicked my warm campaign and then they converted. So 14 people came through this. So $400 came through this campaign as a last click but guess what the first click as far as Facebook data is concerned came from there. So again like if we go into ads manager and we see that these campaigns perform extremely well you know you'll see this often your mid-bottom of funnel was 2x, 3x, 4x return on ad spend and your top of funnel just isn't seemingly performing well you need to come in here and actually get the truth as to what's happening and you know this is actually another interesting part here. So if you look at the tenth most popular path someone clicked into our 1% to 3% lookalike campaign and because we didn't run excludes which I don't do unless you know I'm concerned at the kind of massive audience overlaps then they actually did another click top of funnel and came through my prospecting ads. Now you might look at this and say well if they click this campaign here surely they should be excluded from the top of funnel so they'll only see retargeting ads. In today's tracking world unfortunately we cannot exclude 100% of site visitors. There's many reasons behind that and I'm sure some of them you'll know as part of this training we've covered it but in this case someone has clicked this campaign and then they've clicked this campaign and $300 of revenues come through so that's 10 conversions. The thing is if I just look at Facebook data then phase 3 prospecting interest has performed well and you know you can see the note there it's been scowled out but in reality the first click came from here so that's a problem because if I'm trying to optimize and I say hey this campaign is performing really well this one's not reality is they're performing well together if you take one out they won't perform if you take this one out maybe this one doesn't perform maybe it gets all the attribute sales if you take this one out then maybe this one doesn't get clicks but you need to look at this data and make that decision so you know here for example this is a very common journey for a lot of people is someone clicks top of funnel and then they click bottom of funnel and then they convert so these conversion paths are extremely important for you to understand what's working and how it's working as well so here for example I've looked at conversion path is at the campaign level

you can do the same at the ad set level so if I type in content I can look at the same path but then I can look at the ad set name below it as well so for example in this example here list item number
